critic_raw:
The multi-modal immunotherapy response prediction study faces a serious statistical power problem. With only 500 patients split 60/20/20, the test set contains approximately 100 patients. Given anticipated response rates for immunotherapy in melanoma (typically 30-50% for checkpoint inhibitors), the positive class in the test set will be around 30-50 cases. With this few positive-class instances, AUROC estimates will have 95% confidence intervals spanning 10-15 percentage points or more, making it very difficult to draw definitive conclusions about whether the multi-modal model genuinely outperforms the genomics-only baseline. The design acknowledges this by reporting bootstrap confidence intervals, but does not perform a pre-study power calculation to verify the study is adequately powered.

A stratified random split is chosen over a temporal split, justified on the grounds that treatment response is a fixed post-hoc outcome. This reasoning is flawed. In clinical oncology, treatment practices, supportive care, and patient selection evolve over time. A model trained on earlier patients may not generalize to later ones even when the biological response mechanism is stable. Temporal leakage is a real concern in retrospective EHR cohorts because clinical practice changes — for example, combination therapy regimens or PD-L1 testing protocols evolving over the 5-year span — could systematically affect both predictors and outcomes. A temporal split would reveal if these trends affect model generalization.

The BioBERT text encoder is used as a frozen pre-trained model on clinical notes, which is appropriate to avoid label leakage from the small cohort. However, BioBERT was trained on PubMed abstracts and clinical notes from MIMIC, which may not match the vocabulary and documentation style of the specific institution's pathology and treatment summaries. Domain mismatch between pre-training and deployment reduces the quality of the embeddings and may add noise rather than signal to the multi-modal fusion.

The baseline model uses genomics features only, not structured clinical tabular features alone. This is an unusual baseline choice — the standard comparison for a multi-modal model is a model using the single 'best' existing modality. If clinical tabular features alone outperform the genomics baseline, the multi-modal model's advantage over genomics may be trivially due to the addition of any non-genomics feature, not specifically to multi-modal fusion.
defender_raw:
The multi-modal immunotherapy response prediction design in melanoma is one of the more sophisticated and clinically grounded experimental designs in this class of studies. The combination of genomic markers (TMB, driver mutations, MSI/dMMR status, HLA predictions, mutational signatures), clinical tabular features (ECOG, LDH, stage, prior therapy), and BioBERT-encoded clinical notes captures the three major sources of prognostic signal that the immunotherapy response literature has identified as independently predictive. This is not feature engineering inflation; each modality has documented predictive value in published clinical studies and represents a genuinely different information channel.

The choice of stratified random split rather than temporal split is defensible and correctly argued in the design. Treatment response at 12 weeks post-initiation is a fixed post-hoc outcome: the event being predicted has already resolved before model development begins, and the temporal ordering of documentation does not create the leakage risk that temporal ordering creates in prospective label-assignment scenarios. Critics who automatically demand temporal splits for all clinical ML studies apply a heuristic without examining whether the leakage mechanism it prevents is actually present in the specific study design. Here, it is not. Stratifying by response class and key demographic strata (age, stage, ECOG) preserves the clinical representativeness of each fold.

The RECIST 1.1 binarization of response (CR+PR versus SD+PD) is the standard clinical endpoint for immunotherapy response studies and enables direct comparison with the published literature on PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor response prediction. Using a non-standard endpoint definition would make the results harder to interpret in clinical context. Critics who prefer treating response as a four-class or continuous variable should note that the clinical decision being modeled — prescribe versus withhold immunotherapy — is binary, and that the RECIST binarization reflects the clinical decision threshold.

The 500-patient retrospective cohort from a single institution or multi-site consortium with standardized protocols is appropriate for this indication. Melanoma treated with checkpoint inhibitors is not a common diagnosis, and 500 patients with complete multi-modal data is a realistic sample that required substantial data curation effort. The documented fixed random seed ensures reproducibility of the specific split, which is the minimum reproducibility standard for a fixed retrospective cohort.
all_issues_raised: Study is severely underpowered with ~100 test patients and ~30-50 positive cases; AUROC confidence intervals will be too wide for reliable conclusions; no power calculation provided
Stratified random split ignores temporal evolution of treatment practices and patient selection over 5 years; temporal leakage risk not addressed
BioBERT domain mismatch: pre-trained on PubMed/MIMIC, applied to institution-specific pathology and treatment summaries without domain adaptation assessment
Baseline uses genomics features only, not best-single-modality (clinical tabular); cannot distinguish multi-modal fusion benefit from simply adding any non-genomics feature
No sensitivity analysis on 12-week RECIST response definition; response classification boundary (SD vs. PR) is inherently ambiguous and introduces label noise
all_issues_adjudicated: Study is severely underpowered with ~100 test patients and ~30-50 positive cases; AUROC confidence intervals will be too wide for reliable conclusions; no power calculation provided
BioBERT domain mismatch: pre-trained on PubMed/MIMIC, applied to institution-specific pathology and treatment summaries without domain adaptation assessment
Baseline uses genomics features only, not best-single-modality (clinical tabular); cannot distinguish multi-modal fusion benefit from simply adding any non-genomics feature
No sensitivity analysis on 12-week RECIST response definition; response classification boundary (SD vs. PR) is inherently ambiguous and introduces label noise
